coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and enhancement of carbon quantum dots from Mopan persimmons for Fe3+ sensing and anti-counterfeiting applications
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Haotian
Guan, Li
Chen, Mingjun
Zhang, You
Wu, Yue
Liu, Zhenyang
Wang, Dawei
Wang, Fenghe
Li, Xu
coredata.dc:description: The utilization of biomass is an important approach to supplying sustainable carbon sources for green chemistry, by which the product could be more environmentally friendly and lower toxicity. In this work, the local fruit Mopan persimmons were used to be a carbon source to prepare carbon quantum dots (MP-CQDs) via the hydrothermal method. A series of characteristics were applied to the carbon dots to study the structural and optical properties. The results demonstrated that the MP-CQDs were well synthesized and coated by N, O-related surface functional groups. The PL of MP-CQDs showed an excitation-dependent behavior, whose peak shifted from 446 nm to 545 nm under the excitation wavelength from 350 nm to 450 nm. After a selectivity study on different metal ions, we found that MP-CQDs were sensitive to Fe3+ ions, and the limit of detection (LOD) was determined as 0.324 μM with the 3σ rule. Further, by mixing MP-CQDs with sugar, we successfully applied MP-CQDs to the anti-counterfeiting field by painting anti-counterfeiting patterns with the combination of commercial fluorescent ink (CFI) and MP-CQDs@sugar ink (MPI). The pattern can present different fluorescent properties under 365 nm, 395 nm, and 450 nm respectively. The results revealed that biomass-derived MP-CQDs were a promising candidate for Fe3+ ions detection and anti-counterfeiting application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mopan persimmon
Fe3+ sensing
Quenching mechanism
Aggregation-caused quenching
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The enhancement of MP-CQDs and the application in anti-counterfeiting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM pattern and particle size distribution of MP-CQDs; (b) TEM pattern of a single MP-CQD with its interplanar spacing; (c) TEM diffraction ring pattern; (d) XRD pattern and (e) Raman spectrum of MP-CQDs powder; (f) FT-IR pattern of MP-CQDs aqueous solution; XPS peak separation analysis of (g) C, (h) O and (i) N.

(a) UV–vis, PL, and PLE spectra of MP-CQDs; (b) PL emission spectra of MP-CQDs under different excitation light; (c) fluorescent lifetime decay curve of MP-CQDs; (d) durable test of MP-CQDs when exposed to a 365 nm emission flashlight.

(a) PL emission spectra of MP-CQDs under different pH environments and corresponding photos of MP-CQDs samples under pH = 1, 5, 7, 10, 14; (b) ion-selective study of MP-CQDs; (c) fluorescent intensities of Fe3+ added MP-CQDs samples (from 0 to 500 μM); (d) Fe3+ ion’s quenching efficiency analysis in MP-CQDs solution.

(a) UV–vis spectra of MP-CQDs, Fe3+ and their combination; (b) intensity variations of adding EDTA and Fe3+ consequently and repeatedly into the solution system (λex = 376 nm). (c) a schematic diagram of the on–off-on process in Fe3+ sensing.
S-V curves of MP-CQDs with different Fe3+ concentrations (from 0 to 90 μM) under (a) 15, (b) 25 and (c) 35 °C; (d) the fluorescent lifetime comparation of MP-CQDs and MP-CQDs + Fe3+ (100 μM) sample.

(a) 3D fluorescence spectra of MP-CQDs@sugar; (b) fluorescent spectra of different MP-CQDs concentrations; (c) fluorescent lifetime studies of different MP-CQDs concentrations; (d) fluorescent spectra and corresponding photos of MP-CQDs and MP-CQDs@sugar; (e) fluorescent spectra of different sugar concentrations in 2 mg/ml MP-CQDs aqueous solution; (f) fluorescent lifetime studies of different sugar concentrations in 2 mg/ml MP-CQDs aqueous solution.
photos of the anti-counterfeiting patterns and gelatinoid MP-CQDs@sugar under (a) daylight (b) 365 (c) 395, and (d) 450 nm excitations respectively.
The preparation and applications of MP-CQDs.
Emission, QY, linear range, and LOD of some biomass-derived Fe3+ carbon dots sensors.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and enhancement of carbon quantum dots from Mopan persimmons for Fe3+ sensing and anti-counterfeiting applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hebei Key Laboratory of Photo-Electricity Information and Materials, College of Physics Science and Technology, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Key Laboratory of Photo-Electricity Information and Materials
College of Physics Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hebei Key Laboratory of Photo-Electricity Information and Materials, College of Physics Science and Technology, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of High-precision Computation and Application of Quantum Field Theory of Hebei Province, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of High-precision Computation and Application of Quantum Field Theory of Hebei Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of High-precision Computation and Application of Quantum Field Theory of Hebei Province, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hebei Key Laboratory of Semiconductor Lighting and Display Critical Materials, Hebei Ledphor Optoelectronics Technology Co, LTD, Baoding 071000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Key Laboratory of Semiconductor Lighting and Display Critical Materials
Hebei Ledphor Optoelectronics Technology Co, LTD

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hebei Key Laboratory of Semiconductor Lighting and Display Critical Materials, Hebei Ledphor Optoelectronics Technology Co, LTD, Baoding 071000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The utilization of biomass is an important approach to supplying sustainable carbon sources for green chemistry, by which the product could be more environmentally friendly and lower toxicity. In this work, the local fruit Mopan persimmons were used to be a carbon source to prepare carbon quantum dots (MP-CQDs) via the hydrothermal method. A series of characteristics were applied to the carbon dots to study the structural and optical properties. The results demonstrated that the MP-CQDs were well synthesized and coated by N, O-related surface functional groups. The PL of MP-CQDs showed an excitation-dependent behavior, whose peak shifted from 446 nm to 545 nm under the excitation wavelength from 350 nm to 450 nm. After a selectivity study on different metal ions, we found that MP-CQDs were sensitive to Fe3+ ions, and the limit of detection (LOD) was determined as 0.324 μM with the 3σ rule. Further, by mixing MP-CQDs with sugar, we successfully applied MP-CQDs to the anti-counterfeiting field by painting anti-counterfeiting patterns with the combination of commercial fluorescent ink (CFI) and MP-CQDs@sugar ink (MPI). The pattern can present different fluorescent properties under 365 nm, 395 nm, and 450 nm respectively. The results revealed that biomass-derived MP-CQDs were a promising candidate for Fe3+ ions detection and anti-counterfeiting application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) possess high fluorescent emissions and good stability, and they are waiting to be applied to multiple fields. The selection of carbon sources is important in preparing CDs, and biomass carbon sources from the natural world possess the advantages such as being cheap, non-toxic, extensive, etc. A variety of preparation methods for biomass CDs have been put forward, such as the hydrothermal method [1], microwave method [2], microwave hydrothermal method [3], chemical oxidation method [4], hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) method [5], pyrolysis carbonization method [6] and so on. Researchers have reported many kinds of biomass carbon sources, such as rice husks, coffee grounds, Ginkgo Biloba, and fenugreek seeds, for ions detecting, bio-imaging, electrocatalyst, and electroluminescent [7–10]. Mopan persimmons are very productive in Baoding city, Hebei province, they are cheap and rich in sugar, which is a kind of good biomass carbon source.

Fe3+ is an essential element for the living body and the environment. Both excessive and deficient Fe3+ can harm the biological steady state, which may cause liver cirrhosis, renal failure, heart attack, cancer [11], and iron deficiency anemia (IDA) [12]. Additionally, monitoring the concentration of Fe3+ ions is also important in evaluating the water environment and regulating industry production [13,14]. Though some approaches such as the electrochemical method [15], high resolution-continuum source flame atomic [16], and inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectroscopy [17] have been introduced, they are not simple, cheap, and convenient enough to be broadly applied. Thus, a green, easy-achieve, sensitive, and selective method should be advocated. Biomass-derived CDs have been applied to Fe3+ sensing, such as rice residue [8], Phyllanthus acids [18], bee pollen [19], Lignocellulosic biomass [20] and their limit of detections (LODs) are 0.7462, 0.90, 27 and 15, 0.91 (lowest) µM respectively.

Anti-counterfeiting relates to our daily life tightly. Criminals produce fake banknotes and damage credit systems, illegal factories manufacture imitative commodities and impact the market, irresponsible medical corporations supply counterfeit drugs, and risk consumers [21–23]. Jothi Vinoth Kumar et al. [24] doped Boron into CDs derived from Tribulus Terrestris and printed to stable QR code by the stencil printing method. Al-Qahtani. et al [25] chose straw waste as a carbon source and prepared fluorescent ink, which was non-toxic and stable. Therefore, biomass carbon sources especially native products own huge potential to be transferred into practical CDs.

In this work, MP-CQDs were synthesized from fresh Mopan persimmons by the hydrothermal method. The emission peak of MP-CQDs was located at 458 nm under 376 nm excitation (relative QY = 8.39%), and an excitation-dependent phenomenon was observed. The pH and excitation durable studies indicate the MP-CQDs were stable. The MP-CQDs can be applied for Fe3+ ions detection, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.324 μM (3σ rule). Additionally, MP-CQDs was also applied to anti-counterfeiting ink by combining with sugar compound, which restrained the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) process of MP-CQDs.

Clean persimmons were bought from the local market in Baoding (Hebei, China), and deionized (DI) water was produced by a purification device at Hebei university. Absolute ethyl alcohol, methylene chloride, HCl, and NaOH were purchased from the Damao Chemical Reagent Factory of Tianjin, China; MnCl2, NiSO4·6H2O, Al2(SO4)3·18H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, CaCl2, FeSO4·7H2O, Na2SO4, Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, HgCl2, PbCl2, CuSO4·5H2O, MgSO4, K2SO4, YCl3·6H2O, NdN3O9·6H2O, ZnCl2 and Ethylene Diamine Tetraacetic Acid (EDTA) were obtained from Aladdin Co. ltd (Shanghai, China), which were applied in the selectivity studies of different ions. Sugar was also derived from Aladdin Co. ltd and the commercial fluorescent ink was bought online, which participated in the anti-counterfeiting application. All the chemicals were used without further purification.

Firstly, 5 g of fresh persimmon pulp was smashed in an agate mortar and dispersed in 15 ml DI water. The mixture was transferred into a 25 ml hydrothermal synthesis reactor and heated at the temperature of 150 °C for 4 h in an air-dry oven. After the heating procedure, the pasty outcome was dissolved in 100 ml DI water and centrifuge at 8000 rpm for 10 mins, the supernatant was filtrated with 0.45 μm membranes and condensed to 5 ml by a rotary evaporator. The concentrated solution was separated by silica column chromatography method, and the eluting agent was one-part absolute ethyl alcohol in four-part methylene chloride, then DI water. The former part was considered an impurity, the latter part was collected and named as MP-CQDs. MP-CQDs were freeze-dried to CDs powder and both productions were preserved in a refrigerator.

Quinine sulfate was chosen as the standard reference compound, and the QY of MP-CQDs was calculated as follows [24,25]:
(1)
Φ M
=
Φ S
A S
A M
I M
I S
(
η M
η S
)
2
where M and S represent for MP-CQDs and quinine sulfate solutions; Φ was the fluorescent quantum yields, and the QY of quinine sulfate was 54%; A was the optical densities, to restrain the reabsorption of solutions, UV absorption values were kept between 0.05 and 0.1: MP-CQDs solutions were diluted by water, and quinine sulfate solutions were diluted by 0.1 mol/L H2SO4; I represents for the integrated fluorescent intensity; η is the refractive index of the solvents, and in this case, ηM = ηS = 1.33.

The selecting ions Mn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Na+, Ce3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, K+, Y3+, Nd3+, and Zn2+ were prepared solution with concentration of 1 mmol/L and added to MP-CQDs solution as selectivity study. To determine the LOD of Fe3+ detection and quenching mechanism, a series of different Fe3+ concentration solutions (pH = 3) were prepared under 25 °C, and the concentration of MP-CQDs was set as 2 mg/ml. EDTA was added to wipe off Fe3+ and recycle MP-CQDs, after the recycling, FeCl3·6H2O powder was added and made Fe3+ concentration back to 1 mmol/L. Such a process was repeated five times. Fluorescent spectra (λex = 376 nm, λem = 458 nm) of each sample were measured 5 min after Fe3+ was added, and the blank sample was set as F0.

Sugar was added to 0.8 mg/ml MP-CQDs aqueous solution, which was named MP-CQDs@sugar ink (MPI), and the fluorescent spectra of different sugar concentrations were measured. Luminescence patterns were written by the combination of CFI and MPI (the concentration of sugar was 0.4 g/ml) on a piece of paper and a multi-emission light source was applied to observe the excitation-relying phenomenon. 0.8 g/ml MPI was prepared by adding sugar to 70 °C MP-CQDs aqueous solution, and the high-sugar-concentration MPI was applied to a PMAA mold (engraved by laser, 2 mm deep). The MPI was solidified as a soft solid after cooling down, and a camera was applied to capture the photos.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of MP-CQDs powder was measured by a Bruker D8 Phaser diffractometer, whose working voltage was 40 kV and the radiation source was Cu Κα (λ = 1.0546 Å). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) patterns were derived from the JEM-2100 Plus transmission electron microscope from JEOL. The photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were measured using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer, whose excitation source was a 150 W xenon lamp. The X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) patterns were measured by EscaLab 250 Xi from Thermo Scientific company. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were obtained from Perkin Elmer’s Lambda 950. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured by Spectrum Two from PerkinElmer company. Raman spectrum was derived from the Horiba HR Evolution spectrophotometer. Fluorescent lifetime spectra were measured using a Horiba Fluorolog-3 spectrophotometer. The scanning electron microscope (SEM) pattern was captured by Nova Nano SEM 450 from the FEI company.
The preparation, purification, drying process, Fe3+ sensing and anti-counterfeiting application of MP-CQDs are exhibited in Scheme 1 .

To reveal the structure of as-prepared MP-CQDs, the TEM, HRTEM, XRD, FT-IR, Raman spectrum, and XPS are measured and presented in Fig. 1 . MP-CQDs can disperse well in the aqueous solution, and the average particle diameter is 3.18 ± 0.69 nm (Fig. 1 (a)). The clear crystal lattice stripe can be observed from the HRTEM image patterned in Fig. 1 (b). The d-spacing is 0.21 nm, which originates from the (100) planes of graphite carbon. This result can be further supported by the diffraction pattern of as-prepared CDs (Fig. 1 (c)). A typical 22.7° diffraction peak of amorphous carbon is found in the XRD patterns (Fig. 1 (d)), which also associates with the (100) planes of graphite carbon [26]. Raman spectrum can further reveal the structure and graphitization degree of the MP-CQDs. Generally, the Raman spectrum of MP-CQDs consists of the D band (1336 cm−1) and G band (1550 cm−1), in which the D band represents the structural disorder of the sp2-domain, and the G band represents the existence of the graphite phase [27]. As shown in Fig. 1 (e), the intensity ratio ID/IG = 0.87, which indicates that the MP-CQDs own a stronger graphite degree. For the above reason, Mopan persimmon carbon dots in this work should be classified as CQDs.

The as-prepared MP-CQDs aqueous is stable, and no precipitation occurs for more than one month. The optical properties of MP-CQDs are revealed by the UV–vis, PL, and PLE spectra in Fig. 2 (a). Two shoulders can be found in the absorption spectra located at ∼ 278 and ∼ 329 nm, the 278 nm one originated from the π → π* transition of aromatic C=C, and the 329 nm one is assigned to n → π* transition of aromatic C=O and C=N [7]. The PLE spectrum exhibits a symmetrical broadband peak at 376 nm, and the PL shows an asymmetrical broadband emission peak at 458 nm, whose full width at half maximum (FWHM) is 68.2 and 118.1 nm, and the relative QY is 8.39%. The corresponding photos of MP-CQDs and their freeze-dried powder are shown in Fig. S2 .

The fluorescent lifetime of MP-CQDs is measured under the excitation of a 369 nm LED light source (monitored at 458 nm, Fig. 2 (c)). The decay data can be well fitted by a two-exponential equation with the determination coefficient R2 = 0.99692. The average lifetime τ is 3.868 ns (τ1 = 1.852 ns and τ2 = 5.598 ns), which indicates MP-CQDs own a nanosecond fluorescent lifetime without phosphorescence or afterglow. A durable test of MP-CQDs aqueous solution is carried out under a 365 nm ultraviolet light source, as shown in Fig. 2 (d), the fluorescence intensity decreased to 85% after the excitation of 600 min.

Abundant surface functional groups can give MP-CQDs the potential for ion detection. Real water samples often contain many kinds of metal ions, and a sensor needs to recognize the target ion accurately. The pH value can affect the optical properties of MP-CQDs and the hydrolysis of ions, and MP-CQDs aqueous solutions (2 mg/ml) with different pH values (from 1 to 14) are prepared and their PL spectra are measured. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), pH = 5 makes the emission intensity maximum, and both over acid and basic environment can make the emission intensity decrease, over basic environment may restrain the emission from –COOH functionalization, and the peracid environment may also reduce fluorescence by protonating amine groups and making MP-CQDs aggregation [31]. And the electronic transition of π → π* and n → π* by refilling or depleting the valance bands of graphite nanodomains makes the appearance of MP-CQDs solution becomes darker and browner (with pH increases) [32].

The Al3+, Ca2+, Ce3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Nd3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Y3+, Zn2+ ions are selected to determine the ion selectivity of MP-CQDs, and all the aqueous solutions are adjusted to pH = 3 to restrain the hydrolysis process of ions. The F/F0 values of different ions are shown in Fig. 3 (b), in which F is the fluorescent intensities of the ion-added samples (the ion concentration of each sample is 1 mmol/L), and F0 is the fluorescent intensities of MP-CQDs without any metal ions. The results indicate that Fe3+ reduced the emission of MP-CQDs by 96%, which is the highest value compared to the other metal ions; and Fe3+ can also quench MP-CQDs in other ions environments, as shown in Fig. S4 (the concentrations of the ions are 1 mmol/L), which indicates MP-CQDs own the ability to determine Fe3+ in multi-ion solvents. To further analyze the effect of Fe3+ ions, PL spectra of MP-CQDs with different Fe3+ concentrations are measured and shown in Fig. 3 (c). With the Fe3+ concentration increasing, the fluorescent intensity of MP-CQDs decreases intensely, and the PL spectra have no emission shift (the corresponding photos are shown in Fig. S5
). The relationship between quenching efficiency (F0-F)/F0 and Fe3+ concentration is shown in Fig. 3 (d), a linear relationship (R2 = 0.9864) can be fitted by y = 0.00552x − 0.00559 equation when Fe3+concentration rise from 0 to 90 μM. The LOD can be determined by the 3σ rule, which is based on 3 times of S/N [39,40]:
(2)
LOD =
3 σ
S
where σ is the standard deviation of 11 different standard samples, and S is the slope of the standard curve of MP-CQDs solution’s PL intensity versus Fe3+ concentration. We determine the Fe3+ sensing LOD of MP-CQDs is 0.324 μM, which is good among several typical biomass-derived CDs (Table 1 )[34].

To investigate the quenching mechanism of MP-CQDs in the Fe3+ ion solution, the UV–vis absorption spectra of MP-CQDs, Fe3+, and the mixture of MP-CQDs and Fe3+ solution are measured and shown in Fig. 4 (a). After Fe3+ is added to MP-CQDs solution, the absorption spectra become closer to the absorption of Fe3+, which indicates that Fe3+ ions can absorb excitation photons and lead to quenching. According to previous reports, EDTA can form coordinate bonds with Fe3+, and such a strong affinity can dislodge Fe3+ from carbon dots [41]. To determine the reutilization properties of MP-CQDs, we added EDTA to the quenched MP-CQDs solution (EDTA:Fe3+ = 1:1), and fluorescent intensity recovered by 96% (Fig. 4 (b)), corresponding photos are shown in Fig. S6
. After five times recycling, the fluorescent intensity decreased to 10%, and a schematic diagram of the on–off-on process is shown in Fig. 4 (c). According to the above study, we assume MP-CQDs can be regarded as a kind of clean, cheap, stable, selective, sensitive, and recyclable Fe3+ ion sensor.

The quenching reaction between the quencher and CDs can be generally divided into internal filtering effect (IFE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process, energy transfer, dynamic quenching, and static quenching [42]. In a static quenching process, the quencher and CDs generate coordination compounds and these new substances make CDs lose their fluorescent properties. The Stern-Volmer equation is important to distinguish between dynamic quenching and static quenching [26,43]:
(3)
F 0
F
=
τ 0
τ
= 1 +
K
SV
Q
= 1 +
K q
τ
av
[ Q ]
where τ0 and τ are the fluorescent lifetimes of the blank and quenched MP-CQDs samples respectively, KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant, Kq is the constant of dynamic quenching rate (maximum value in dynamic quenching is about 2 × 1010 L/(mol·s)), τav is the average fluorescent lifetime of fluorophores (generally τav is at the magnitude of 10-8 ns), [Q] represents for the concentration of quencher.

Fig. 5 (a) (b) (c) show the S-V curves of F0/F versus different Fe3+ concentrations from 0 to 90 μM under 15, 25, and 35 °C respectively. According to Eq. (3), the slopes of S-V curves are the Stern-Volmer constants KSV , which are 9.58 × 103, 8.20 × 103, and 4.46 × 103 L/mol respectively. Because τav is at the magnitude of 10-8 ns, each Kq is much larger than the maximum dynamic quenching value (2 × 1010 L/(mol·s)), which can determine the static quenching process to a degree. Further, as the temperature varies from 15 to 35 °C, KSV decrease from 9.58 × 103 to 4.46 × 103 L/mol, which also confirms the characteristic of static quenching, because the increasing temperature can break down the formed coordination compounds. Thirdly, the value of τ0 and τ basically remains the same (3.868 and 3.845 ns respectively), which is another characteristic of static quenching, because the quenching does not affect the lifetime of the excitation state. Above all, the quenching behavior of MP-CQDs is determined to be static quenching, MP-CQDs and Fe3+ constitute ground state complex and effective fluorescent MP-CQDs become less ( Fig. S7 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The enhancement of MP-CQDs and the application in anti-counterfeiting
ce:section.ce:para: Anti-counterfeiting is important in people’s activities such as transaction, medication, and production. Researchers have been studying characteristic, high fluorescent, malleable, easy-obtained, and environmental anti-counterfeiting materials for many years. To apply MP-CQDs to the anti-counterfeiting field, we added sugar to MP-CQDs and the MP-CQDs@Sugar compound is obtained. The optical properties of the as-prepared compound are measured and shown in Fig. 6 . The 3D PL spectrum of MP-CQDs@sugar under different excitation wavelengths is provided in Fig. 6 (a), which indicates that the excitation-dependent behavior still exists after sugar is added to the MP-CQDs solution. Remarkably, when the PL peak varies from 456 to 529 nm, the fluorescent intensity is only reduced by 10%.

The concentration and fluorescence lifetime studies of MP-CQDs aqueous solutions are shown in Fig. 6 (b) and (c). When the concentration of MP-CQDs varies from 0.5 to 5 mg/ml, the emission peaks shift red for 13 nm (from 459 to 472 nm), and the fluorescence lifetime decrease for 1.17 ns (from 4.31 to 3.14 ns), which conform to the typical characteristics of ACQ process: aggregated MP-CQDs trigger energy level splitting and make bandgap decrease, then enhance non-radioactive transition and reduce the fluorescent intensity [44]. To restrain the ACQ process, we introduced sugar into the solution system, the emission intensity was largely enhanced (by 81% with 0.7 g/ml sugar, Fig. 6 (d)) and the QY was increased to 13.74%. As shown in Fig. 6 (e) and (f), when the concentration of sugar is increased from 0 to 0.7 g/ml, the emission peaks shift blue for 11 nm (from 467 to 456 nm), and the fluorescence lifetime prolonged for 1.73 ns (from 3.28 to 5.01 ns). Sugar stabled the energy levels and enhanced the emission of MP-CQDs aqueous solution by anti-ACQ process, a mechanism diagram is shown in Fig. S8 and the lifetime data are shown in Table S1 and Table S2 .

After the enhancement of MP-CQDs with sugar, we obtained a well-performance fluorescent ink MPI. We applied MPI to an anti-counterfeiting pattern by combining MPI and CFI, as shown in Fig. 7 (a) (b) (c) (d), the combination code is written on a piece of paper, due to the characteristic of excitation-dependent, the color of MPI painted numbers can change from blue to yellow when excitation source varies from 365 nm to 450 nm, while CFI keeps emitting blue light till extinguishing. Further, high-concentration MPI is malleable and can be made to mold with specified shapes, hot viscous MPI can be transferred to soft solid after being placed into a PMAA mold, and the solid-state luminescence still maintains excitation-dependent characteristics ( Fig. S9 ).

In summary, we have successfully applied the hydrothermal method and column chromatography method to prepare MP-CQDs. MP-CQDs can be well dispersed in the aqueous solution and own a blue emission of 458 nm under a 376 nm excitation (relative QY = 8.39%). The excitation-dependent phenomenon was observed, which originated from surface groups and edge states of MP-CQDs. MP-CQDs are sensitive to Fe3+ ion (LOD is 0.324 μM according to the 3σ rule) and can be recycled by EDTA, which indicates MP-CQDs are good Fe3+ sensors; After we discussed Kq , S-V fitting curves and fluorescent lifetime decays, the reaction between MP-CQDs and Fe3+ was determined to be a static quenching. To apply MP-CQDs to anti-counterfeiting patterns, we added sugar to the MP-CQDs water solution, which restrained the ACQ effect and enhanced excitation-dependent emission. We painted anti-counterfeiting number codes on a piece of paper with the combination of MPI and CFI, which shows different fluorescent properties under 365, 395, and 450 nm excitation light sources respectively. Further, high-concentration MPI is malleable, solid-state emissions can take place after MPI solidifies in a PMAA mold.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partly funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61974009, No. 62075055, No. 62175075), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. 2020201030), the Central Project Guide local science and technology for development of Hebei Province (216Z1104G, 226Z1103G), and Innovation and entrepreneurship training program for college students (2022178).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.139906.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tailoring surface groups of carbon quantum dots to improve photoluminescence behaviors
Appl. Surf. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Tian, S. Hu, L. Wu, Q. Chang, J. Yang, J. Liu, Tailoring surface groups of carbon quantum dots to improve photoluminescence behaviors, Applied Surface Science 301 (2014) 156-160. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2014.02.028.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A green heterogeneous synthesis of N-doped carbon dots and their photoluminescence applications in solid and aqueous states
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Xu, G. He, Z. Li, F. He, F. Gao, Y. Su, L. Zhang, Z. Yang, Y. Zhang, A green heterogeneous synthesis of N-doped carbon dots and their photoluminescence applications in solid and aqueous states, Nanoscale 6(17) (2014) 10307-15. https://doi.org/10.1039/c4nr02792b.
